summary: Cantrill reflects on the open sourcing of Solaris and the crucial distinction between having the legal right to fork versus the practical power to do so. He argues that OpenSolaris failed to achieve true openness because essential bits remained closed and Sun monopolized the engineering talent, leaving the community unable to actually exercise forking rights. The announcement of the Illumos project marks a turning point, as it provides open-sourced alternatives to those final closed components, finally giving the community real power to fork.
key_points: The right to fork is meaningless without the practical power to fork
OpenSolaris never reached 100% openness due to legal and historical constraints
Sun's monopoly on Solaris engineering talent starved the community of resources
The Illumos project represents a critical milestone for true open source independence
money_quotes: If there's one thing we've learned from watching Linux, it's to not become forkophobic.
Paradoxically, in an environment where forks are actively encouraged (e.g. Linux) forking seems to be less of a problem than in environments where forking is viewed as apostasy (e.g. BSD).
Even though the right to fork was understood, there was not enough attention paid to the power to fork.
The community was entirely deprived of the oxygen that it would have needed to exercise its right to fork.
That power is absolutely essential for the vitality of any open source community.
key_insight: True open source freedom requires not just the legal right to fork, but the practical capability—including access to all essential code and a distributed talent pool—to actually do so.
